findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 2

findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 4, Slide 8, Slide 16

findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 13

findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 12, Slide 14, Slide 18

findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 15

findings.label: Does the deck include a Competition or Competitive Landscape slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 7

findings.label: Does the deck have at least 8 slides?
findings.evidence: Deck has 19 slides (minimum 8)

findings.label: Does the deck contain any specific revenue figures, ARR, MRR, or financial projections?
findings.evidence: Revenue/currency figures found

findings.label: Does the deck provide a source, citation, or methodology for its TAM/SAM/SOM figures? A named research firm, report title, or calculation methodology counts. Vague phrases like 'industry data' do not count.
findings.evidence: ¹ Grand View Research: Global solar thermal market $32B (2025) ² IEA Net Zero Scenario: High-insolation regions ~$6-8B subset ³ GCC/MENA cumulative opportunity 2026-2030 ⁴ Ceiling scenario assuming full mandate enforcement

findings.label: Does the deck provide evidence of customer discovery or validated demand — such as interviews, surveys, letters of intent, waitlists, or pilot results?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've identified a real market problem and sized it well, but investors will want to know you've actually talked to the people who need this — add a slide showing 3-4 specific customer conversations (even brief ones) that validate the pain points you're describing, like "spoke with 12 villa developers in Dubai who cited maintenance costs as their #1 reason for avoiding solar hot water" or "pilot with [developer name] showed 40% reduction in system failures over 6 months." This doesn't need to be a full case study yet, just concrete evidence that builders and developers are actively frustrated enough to consider a new solution.

findings.label: Does the deck clearly define the target customer segment with specific characteristics — not just broad demographics?
findings.evidence: UAE Villas & Developers — $400M Segment + Flat-roof architecture dominant → ideal fit for SolarisKit GCC + MENA Focus market

findings.label: Does the deck explain why this problem is urgent or why now is the right time to solve it?
findings.evidence: Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel), Rising electricity demand from water heating, Strong push for decarbonisation (e.g. UAE Climate Law), Large and growing pipeline of new villa developments
findings.suggestion: You've got the market drivers listed, but they feel like a checklist rather than a timeline—add a specific regulatory deadline or enforcement date that creates urgency for developers right now. For example, if Estidama requires all new villas to have functioning solar hot water systems by Q3 2025, or if Dubai's building code tightens in 2026 with penalties for non-compliance, that's the "why now" that makes developers scramble to find a reliable solution like yours instead of waiting.

findings.label: Does the deck address any regulatory or compliance factors relevant to the market?
findings.evidence: Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel); Solar hot water mandated by regulation; Strong push for decarbonisation (e.g. UAE Climate Law)

findings.label: Does the deck claim AI, ML, or proprietary technology? If so, does it provide any technical substance — architecture, methodology, or differentiation — beyond marketing language?
findings.evidence: HelioHelp - AI Deployment Layer: Automated system design and sizing, AI-guided installation and troubleshooting, Performance and carbon modelling, Knowledge engine for global installers and partners. Proprietary design and performance modelling. In development — v1.0 targeted Q3 2026
findings.suggestion: Right now HelioHelp reads like a feature wishlist—you're describing what it will do, not how it works or why it's hard to replicate. Add one concrete technical detail that shows you've actually solved a real problem: for example, "HelioHelp's proprietary algorithm accounts for seasonal demand variance and hard-water scaling patterns specific to GCC climates, reducing oversizing errors by ~30% compared to standard solar design tools" or explain the specific data inputs (thermal load modeling, weather patterns, building envelope data) that feed your AI engine and why competitors can't easily access them. This transforms HelioHelp from "we'll build an AI tool" into "we've built something

findings.label: Does the deck explain any defensible moat — IP, patents, proprietary data, network effects, or switching costs?
findings.evidence: Patented collector technology (US, EU, UK); Purpose-built for flat-roof GCC architecture (structural niche); Hard-water polymer design → long-term reliability advantage; Flat-pack manufacturing → cost and logistics advantage; Proprietary design and performance modelling; Installer knowledge platform; Deployment data and system optimisation; As deployments grow → data, installer network, and platform value increase → defensibility strengthens.

findings.label: Does the deck describe the product's current development stage — idea, prototype, beta, live, or scaling?
findings.evidence: Full retrofit: 570 solar collector system deployment (underway). + Delivery underway — Q2 2026

findings.label: Does the deck describe a clear and logical customer journey or product workflow?
findings.evidence: Land → Expand → Scale model. Hotels, utilities, and developers validate reliability and ROI. Projects convert into repeat deployments through developer frameworks and retrofit programmes. Utility and ESCO partners deploy across large building portfolios.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any third-party technology dependencies that could represent a risk — cloud providers, APIs, open-source components?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a brief section to your Technology slide that names the cloud infrastructure, APIs, or third-party services powering HelioHelp — for example, "HelioHelp runs on AWS with integrations to weather APIs and satellite imagery providers for real-time solar irradiance modeling." Investors will ask this in due diligence, and naming your dependencies now (even if you're still evaluating vendors) shows you've thought through operational resilience and vendor lock-in risks.

findings.label: Does the deck address data privacy, security, or compliance relevant to the product?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You're operating in a regulated market (solar thermal systems, hot water mandates, building codes across GCC) where compliance and data security will be investor questions—add one slide in the Appendix addressing this directly. Show that your AI platform (HelioHelp) handles customer data securely, that your hardware meets relevant thermal/electrical safety standards (ISO certifications you mention), and that you're compliant with UAE/GCC data residency or privacy requirements if applicable—even a simple statement like "ISO 9001 certified manufacturing, data stored in-region, compliant with UAE Data Protection Law" gives investors confidence you've thought this through rather than leaving them to assume you haven't.

findings.label: Do the financial projections include stated assumptions — growth rate basis, customer acquisition assumptions, or cost structure logic?
findings.evidence: The slides show financial projections with revenue figures and scenarios (Bear/Base/Bull) but do not explicitly state growth rate assumptions, customer acquisition assumptions, or detailed cost structure logic. The slides mention 'Conservative projections with base/conservative scenarios' and reference 'GTM via developers/ESCOs' but do not provide the underlying mathematical assumptions or detailed cost breakdowns that would constitute stated assumptions.
findings.suggestion: Add a one-slide assumptions sheet that shows the math behind your 2030 projections: spell out your customer acquisition ramp (e.g., "50 units 2026 → 200 units 2027 → 500 units 2028"), your average selling price per unit, and how you're building from the $213K pipeline to $5.3M base case revenue. For example, if you're assuming 500 villa retrofits at $10K each plus licensing from one OEM partner at $50K annually by 2028, state that explicitly—investors need to see whether they believe your unit growth assumptions, not just the final number.

findings.label: Does the deck state the current burn rate and runway in months?
findings.evidence: $350K seed funding: 18-month cash runway

findings.label: Does the deck provide unit economics — CAC, LTV, LTV:CAC ratio, or payback period?
findings.evidence: Unit Economics: Hotels 38–46% and Villas 35–43%; Rixos Hotel ROI: 3.1 years
findings.suggestion: You've got gross margins and one payback period (Rixos at 3.1 years), but investors need to see the full unit economics story—specifically CAC and LTV for your core customer segments (hotels vs. villas) so they can understand how quickly you recover customer acquisition costs. Add a simple table on Slide 14 showing: for hotels, what's your average CAC (installation + sales costs per project), what's the LTV (total profit from that customer over their lifetime with you), and what's the payback period; then do the same for villas, since your margins differ (38–46% vs. 35–43%) and the economics likely do too.

findings.label: Are there any numbers that appear inconsistent across different slides — for example revenue figures that don't match between summary and detail slides?
findings.evidence: Slide 12 shows 'CONTRACTED & DEPLOYING - $213K (Q2 2026 delivery)' with total $213,000. Slide 14 shows 2026 Hardware Revenue as '$0.3M' which equals $300,000, creating a discrepancy of $87,000 between the contracted amount and projected hardware revenue.
findings.suggestion: The $213K contracted on Slide 12 is your near-term, locked-in revenue—keep that separate and clear. On Slide 14, your 2026 hardware revenue of $0.3M should explicitly call out that this includes the $213K Rixos + EtihadWE deals plus an additional $87K from new customer wins you expect to close by year-end, so investors see how the contracted pipeline scales into your full-year projection rather than wondering where the gap comes from.

findings.label: Does the deck explain what the investment will be used for — with a specific breakdown of use of funds?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide showing exactly how you'll deploy this new funding round—break it down by category (e.g., "Manufacturing scale-up: $X," "Sales & installation team: $X," "R&D for licensing platform: $X," "Working capital: $X") with a timeline showing when each dollar gets spent over the next 18 months. Right now investors see your $350K seed runway but have no visibility into what this new raise will fund, which makes it hard for them to assess whether the amount matches your ambitions.

findings.label: Does the deck mention the path to profitability or breakeven point?
findings.evidence: EBITDA positive 2027

findings.label: Does the deck provide evidence of paying customers — not just users, pilots, or sign-ups?
findings.evidence: Rixos (live project) and EtihadWE listed under revenue streams, but no explicit confirmation of payment or revenue received
findings.suggestion: Add a single line under Rixos and EtihadWE stating the payment status and timing — for example, "Rixos: $180K received Q3 2024" or "EtihadWE: Payment received upon installation, $45K to date" — so investors see actual cash in the door, not just project names.

findings.label: Does the deck describe a specific, credible go-to-market strategy — not just 'sales and marketing'?
findings.evidence: MANUFACTURE MONETISE SCALE: UAE Factory Revenue Market Ras Al Khaimah Streams Routes with DIRECT SALES (HOTELS / RESORTS / PROPERTY DEVELOPERS), UTILITIES, LICENSING (6-12% royalties, Regional rights, Rheem MEA (MOU signed), Regional distributors), and GLOBAL INSTALLATION PARTNERS (Low capex scaling)

findings.label: Does the deck show month-over-month or year-over-year growth with specific numbers?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've got great partnerships and unit economics here, but investors need to see your actual growth trajectory to believe this is working. Add specific numbers showing how revenue or units sold have grown month-over-month or year-over-year—for example, if you shipped 500 units in January and 750 in February, or if Rixos generated $80K in Q1 and $120K in Q2, that proves the model is accelerating and gives investors confidence in your scaling claims.

findings.label: Does the deck mention customer retention, churn rate, or NPS?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Your Rixos case study is powerful proof of durability, but investors will want to know if customers actually stick around—add a line showing the hotel renewed or extended their contract after the storm, or mention that your O&M contracts have X% renewal rates, so they see this isn't a one-off sale but recurring revenue with staying power.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any named customer logos, case studies, or testimonials?
findings.evidence: Rixos (live project), EtihadWE

findings.label: Does the deck name specific competitors — not just generic categories like 'traditional players' or 'legacy solutions'?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Name 2–3 actual competitors in the GCC market instead of "Legacy Solar" and "Heat Pumps"—for example, replace the chart with something like "SolarisKit vs. Sunrun's flat-roof offering vs. Daikin heat pumps vs. Tesla Powerwall" so investors immediately understand who you're actually beating and can validate your claims against real players they know.

findings.label: Does the competitive analysis appear superficial — for example a magic quadrant where the company leads in all dimensions without explanation?
findings.evidence: SolarisKit is marked with strong advantage (●) across nearly all dimensions: Flat-Roof Suitability, Power Consumption, Energy Savings, Hard Water Resistance, Overheating Protection, Maintenance Complexity, and Storm/Wind Resilience, while competitors are marked with weaknesses (○) or conditional marks (▲), with no explanation provided for why SolarisKit achieves superiority across all categories

findings.label: Does the deck address the threat of large incumbents or well-funded competitors entering the space?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Your comparison table shows why SolarisKit wins against current alternatives, but it doesn't address what happens when a major player like Bosch, Lennox, or a well-funded solar company decides to build exactly what you're building. Add a slide that shows your defensibility: what's your 18-month moat? For example, you could highlight "Proprietary polymer absorber formulation (patent filed Q2 2024) + GCC flat-roof installation IP developed through 200+ deployments — competitors would need 2+ years and $5M+ to replicate." This tells investors you're not just first, you're built to stay ahead.

findings.label: Does the team have a gap in a critical function — for example no technical co-founder for a tech product, or no sales lead for a B2B company?
findings.evidence: To be recruited (post raise) and Recruited appear in the organizational chart for Product Development Engineer and Process Electrician Worker positions
findings.suggestion: You've got strong domain expertise and sales leadership locked in, but you're asking investors to fund a company where your Product Development Engineer and Process Electrician Worker—roles critical to actually building and scaling your product—are still question marks. Replace "To be recruited" with the name and one-line credential of someone already committed to joining, even if part-time during their notice period, or explicitly state you're hiring a specific person (e.g., "Sarah Chen, former manufacturing lead at [company], joining September 2026"). This shows you can execute on your post-raise plan, not just hope to find the right people later.

findings.label: Does the deck provide relevant prior experience for each founder — domain expertise, prior startups, or industry background?
findings.evidence: Chartered mechanical engineer with 20 years of experience in solar thermal technology gained from industry, academia, and enterprise. Highly experienced in solar thermal technology, manufacturing, and quality. +Launched multiple products into the Australian solar thermal market generating >$10M AUD in revenue +Scotland's inventor of the year in 2021 +Previous Enterprise Fellow Royal Society of Edinburgh and winner of Net Zero Edge Prize, COP26, Glasgow

findings.label: Does the deck mention any advisors, board members, or notable backers with relevant expertise?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've got strong individual credentials, but you're missing a chance to anchor investor confidence—add 2-3 specific advisors or board members with relevant expertise to the slide, ideally someone with solar/cleantech scaling experience or a recognizable name in the space. For example, you could add a line like "Advisory Board: [Name], former VP of Product at [major solar company] | [Name], cleantech investor at [fund]" below the team table, which signals you've brought in experienced guides for the execution phase ahead.

findings.label: Does the deck mention founder vesting schedules or equity arrangements?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a single line to your Team slide stating the vesting schedule for key founders—something like "Dr. Faisal Ghani: 4-year vest with 1-year cliff" alongside his bio. This shows investors you're aligned for the long haul and removes a question they'll ask in every meeting, letting you spend time on what makes your team special instead.

findings.label: Does the deck operate in a regulated industry — fintech, healthtech, edtech, legaltech — without mentioning relevant licences, compliance, or regulatory approvals?
findings.evidence: Solar hot water is mandated across the UAE — yet adoption remains limited due to poor system reliability and climate mismatch. + Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel)
findings.suggestion: You've mentioned Solar Keymark certification on slide 3, but investors in regulated markets need to see your full compliance roadmap mapped to the specific mandates you're chasing—add a slide showing which certifications you hold (Solar Keymark ✓), which you're pursuing (Dubai Municipality approval, Estidama compliance, etc.), and your timeline to each, since Estidama and Barjeel are literally listed as market drivers on slide 6 but never addressed as gating items. For example: "Solar Keymark: Complete | Dubai Municipality Technical Approval: Q2 2026 | Estidama Compliance: Q3 2026 | Barjeel Standard

findings.label: Does the deck mention any pending litigation, IP disputes, or legal encumbrances?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've got patents locked in three jurisdictions and a strong IP moat, but investors will want explicit clarity on whether any competitors or former partners are challenging your patents or claiming overlapping rights—add a single sentence to your IP slide stating something like "No pending patent disputes or IP challenges; all three jurisdictions (US, EU, UK) grants finalized with no opposition periods active" to remove any ambiguity and signal clean legal standing.

findings.label: Are there any numerical inconsistencies across slides — for example revenue figures that contradict each other, or growth rates that do not match the underlying numbers?
findings.evidence: Slide 1 states '$207K Project Contract Value' while Slide 4 states 'PO Value: AED760K ($207K USD)' — these align. However, Slide 6 presents market sizing figures ($32B TAM, $400M SAM, $2B SOM segment) that cannot be verified against other slides for internal consistency, and the relationship between '5% UAE market share = $20M annual revenue opportunity' and the $400M SAM figure is unclear without additional context.
findings.suggestion: On Slide 6, add a single line showing how the 5% UAE market share ($20M) connects to your $400M SAM — for example: "5% UAE market share of $400M SAM = $20M annual revenue opportunity" or show the calculation explicitly so investors see the math is intentional, not disconnected. Right now it reads like two separate claims, and investors will pause to verify the relationship themselves.

findings.label: Does the deck make any extraordinary claims — market leadership, unique technology, or exceptional growth — without supporting evidence?
findings.evidence: The world's first flat-packed solar collector

findings.label: Is the deck professionally presented — consistent formatting, no spelling errors, no placeholder text?
findings.evidence: Multiple instances of incomplete text: 'Strong push for dec' (Slide 6 cuts off mid-word), '[URL_REDACTED]' placeholders appear throughout, and Slide 3 labeled '[UNKNOWN]' suggests incomplete slide deck
findings.suggestion: Go through the entire deck systematically and replace every instance of [URL_REDACTED] and [UNKNOWN] with actual content — Slide 6 cuts off mid-sentence at "Strong push for dec" (should be "decarbonisation"), and Slide 3's title needs to be filled in properly. Run a final spell-check pass on all slides, then export as PDF and open it fresh to catch any remaining formatting breaks or orphaned text that might have been missed.
